name: Acasia
description:
Acasia

Key Demographic: Human

Region Diagnostic Summary

Current Hook

The Empire conquered it first. The Empire left it last. Now neither remembers why.

Core Conflict

Players are caught between the Margravine's iron law, the Lame King's desperate legitimacy, and the Curse that devours broken promises.

The One Thing Only This Region Does

Geometric Haunting: Roads loop, bridges demand tolls in memory, and contracts signed on bridges become physically binding curses.

The Ninth Taboo Manifestation

In any Acasian scene, the ninth toll paid at a bridge is never the last. The wise pay eight and barter for the ninth. The ninth toll is collected by a child with no name. The child is the Curse's enforcer.

The Price

Players sacrifice memory, identity, or future labor to gain power. Every reward requires a gamble: memory loss, Hollow attention, Obligation, or Corruption.

Faction Triad

Faction in the Realm:

|p0.25|p0.25|p0.15|}

Faction

GoalMethodWhy players care
Margravine of the Broken MarchKeep the province from fracturing completelyIron law, feud tallies, blood-oathsShe holds the only valid Imperial seals
The Lame KingBe recognized as a true sovereignTraveling throne, debtor armies, decreesHe controls the roads the Margravine cannot
Free CompaniesFind a permanent contract before winterSelling loyalty, swapping colors mid-marchThey are the only law between the claimants

Quick Reference

Tagline: The Empire conquered it first. The Empire left it last. Now neither remembers why.

Margravine of the Broken March (Elite):

“The tithe must be paid, not merely offered. A promise written in ash and sealed with iron will is worth more than a thousand gold coins scattered on the wind. The Empire is dead. Its weight is not.”

Hedge-Witch (Commoner):

“Don't cross the Pale Causeway at dusk, especially not if you've got a silver tooth. The bridge'll remember it, and next time you pass, it'll want more than just your footfall. In Acasia, every road has a memory. The question is who pays the toll. The Empire forgot. That's why it fell.”

Genre / Mood: Low-fantasy wasteland, mercenary realism, cursed borderlands.

Starting Location: The Pale Causeway's last inn, walls soot-blackened by coal and a century of gossip. A bounty poster flutters beside a half-empty hearth. The innkeeper is a one-eyed woman who pours small beer without asking. A stranger at the bar offers a sealed contract: retrieve a dowry chest from a Blackwood matriarch before the Margravine's feast day. “The chest contains a claim, not coin,” the stranger whispers. “But a claim in Acasia is worth more than gold—it is a key to a bridge that does not loop. The Empire's surveyors marked it. Then they died.”

Theme & Atmosphere

Acasia was the first province the Utaran Empire swallowed when it turned its eyes east. The legions marched through the Pale Causeway, crushed the hill-kings, and planted their eagles in the Blackwood. For centuries, Acasia was the empire's eastern breadbasket and its first line of defense. Grain flowed west; coin flowed east. The roads were straight, the bridges paid, and the Curse was just a farmer's whisper.

Then the empire rotted from within. When the last Utaran emperor died and the provinces broke away, Acasia lingered for another hundred years — a ghost province whose governors still stamped Utaran seals, whose courts still cited Utaran law, whose coins still bore the emperor's face. No one came from the west to collect the taxes. No one sent legions to enforce the peace. The Acasians kept the forms, because they had never learned to keep anything else. They had no native king, no unifying faith, no common tongue beyond the legionary's Utaran. They were the empire's children, and when the empire died, they became orphans.

For a century they clung to the ruins. Then the plagues came. Then the famines. Then the Curse — a slow rot of roads that led nowhere, bridges that looped back on themselves, villages that woke to find their neighbors' houses standing empty. The old imperial surveys became maps of places that no longer existed. The grain blights turned the storehouses to dust. The free companies, once hired to keep the peace, became the only law.

That was two hundred years ago.

Now Acasia is a wound that will not close. It sits between Ecktoria to the west and Vhasia to the east, claimed by both, ruled by neither. Petty kings raise their banners in burned keeps. Free Companies trade loyalty like grain, swapping colors mid-march. The Margravine of the Broken March keeps a tally of feuds, binding bloodlines to her feast days. The Lame King travels on a throne carried by debtors, making law by pointing. And the Curse — the nameless thing that makes crossroads turn back on themselves — is not a myth. It is the weight of broken promises, and Acasia is drowning in them.

South, on the marshy islands of the Amaranthine coast, the city of Silkstrand squats on reclaimed silt, trading in silk, silver, and secrets. The Acasians call it “the Sink” — a place where the Curse thins, but where law is measured in coin, not custom. Few Acasians go there. Fewer return unchanged.

What you notice first: The smell of mildew on old parchment and wet ash. The way every milestone has been recarved — first in Utaran, then in a half-dozen failed scripts. The silence of a village that heard you coming and chose not to open its gates.

The one rule that kills newcomers: Never sign a contract on a bridge. The river is a witness, and it never forgets a signature. The Empire's last governor signed a treaty on the Pont-du-Tithe. No one knows what it said. The bridge remembers.

Regional Mood: Famine, Ambition, and the Ghost of Empire

Here, the past is not a memory — it is a geometry. The Utaran Empire left behind roads that lead to nowhere, laws that contradict each other, and a hunger that no harvest can fill. Acasia's petty kings hold court in burned keeps, their crowns forged from iron nails pulled from famine coffins. The Margravine keeps a tally of feuds, binding bloodlines to her feast days. Free Companies trade loyalty like grain, swapping colors mid-march. And beneath it all, the Curse waits. It is not a monster. It is a wrong turn: the bridge that leads back to the same shore, the milestone that faces the wrong direction, the bell that tolls for no funeral. In Acasia, the land itself has become a broken promise, and the fine print is written in mildew.

The Curse is not ancient. It is a legacy. Some say it began with the last Imperial Survey, when the surveyors tried to map every bridge, every ford, every toll—and in the measuring, broke something. Others say it was the plagues, when the dead were buried in unmarked pits and the priests forgot the proper rites. The hedge-witches say it was the empire's own fault: they ruled Acasia for centuries but never taught the Acasians to rule themselves. When the empire left, the land had no king, no law, no memory. The Curse filled the gap.

Now the Margravine and the Lame King fight over the scraps, while free companies sell their swords to whichever claimant pays first. The hedge-witches tie red thread around bridges and leave offerings of salt at crossroads, hoping to keep the Curse from spreading. The villagers pray to saints the Empire forgot. And the roads — the old imperial roads — still march west toward a capital that no longer exists. The capital does not answer. But the roads keep marching.

The Curse of Acasia (Campaign Timer )

The Curse is a slow accumulation of broken promises and unpaid tolls. Track it with a visible timer:

Curse Awakening [10] – Advances when:

  • A contract is broken on a bridge (+1)
  • A character speaks the ninth word in a negotiation (+1)
  • The party ignores a road omen (GM spends 1 SB to tick +1)
  • A free company flips allegiance mid-contract (+2)

At 4 segments: Navigation rolls in Acasia gain +1 DV (roads shift).

At 8 segments: The Curse Stirrs complication triggers once per session (roll on the table below).

At 10 segments: The Curse manifests as a physical threat — a bridge eats a village, a road swallows a patrol, or the ghost of the last emperor rides.

Curse Stirrs (d6)

When the timer reaches 8, roll 1d6 for an immediate complication:

  1. A milestone points the wrong way. Navigation DV +2 for the scene.
  2. A bridge you crossed earlier now loops back to where you started. Lose 1 segment of progress.
  3. A toll collector's ghost demands payment in a currency that no longer exists. Pay 1 Boon or mark 1 Fatigue.
  4. A child with no shadow offers directions. If you follow, you arrive at your destination — but you forget why you came.
  5. The Margravine's seal cracks. One of your Diamonds (writ, charter, token) becomes a forgery until you re-witness it.
  6. The Hollow's breath freezes the ford. Crossing requires a Desperate roll; on a Miss, you lose 1 Supply (drowned).

Prominent NPCs of Acasia

Name

Role/TitleMotivationRumored Quirk
Zara “the Ledger”Margravine of the Broken MarchKeep the province from fracturing completelyShe has never lost a feud; her sword is also a counting rod. She claims descent from the last Utaran governor.
Oren “Tin Crown”The Lame KingBe recognized as a true sovereignHis traveling throne is carried by debtors; he pays them in promises. His grandfather was a free company captain who declared himself king.
YsabeauHedge-Witch of the Blackwood CharcoalersSurvive the Curse and keep her kin from the ropeShe knows which bridges eat travelers, and her price is a story that ends badly. Her grandmother witnessed the last imperial survey.
The BrideDowry chest claimant (Blackwood matriarch's heir)Recover the chest before the Margravine's feast dayShe carries a dagger in her wedding veil. The claim is to a bridge that drowned. The bridge was built by the Utaran legions.
VladanFree Company Captain (“The Rusted Banner”)Find a permanent contract before winterHe has served three kings this year. He cannot remember their names. His company's banner is an Utaran eagle, torn and stitched.
Sister AshEx-Imperial surveyorFinish the old map of AcasiaShe visits villages that no longer exist. The map shows them anyway. She carries a sealed chest of the last governor's papers.

Names of Acasia

Inspired by the martial duchies of a fallen southern empire: clipped Utaran, war-names, and the hard consonants of mountain keeps. Many families still bear Utaranized surnames, though they have not spoken the language in generations.

(Pass/Ruin/Toll-Town)

Broken milestone on the old Imperial Road; borders “moved” overnight. Vine-terrace hillside with an abandoned press; strangler ivy owns it now.
